topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered as I pulled the thick envelope out. It was a card, crisp white stock, completely blank save for a single, elegantly scripted line: "Happy Birthday." My hand, already trembling slightly, felt clumsy as I unfolded it. Every muscle in my neck tightened. I stared at the paper, the silence in my small apartment suddenly amplified, heavy and suffocating. Who would send this? And why? A prickle of unease crawled up my arms, cold and insistent. I quickly folded it, shoving it deep into a drawer, the sound of the latch clicking echoing too loudly in the quiet room. I kept glancing towards the front door, half-expecting someone to materialize in the hallway.

[story 2]

I snatched the mail from the box with a jerky movement, my breath catching in my throat. There, tucked between a utility bill and a flyer, was a card. Unsigned. The ink looked fresh, the paper pristine. My jaw went slack for a moment, and I could feel a sudden, sharp tension radiating from my chest. My heart began to thump a frantic rhythm against my ribs. I picked it up, my fingers brushing against the unfamiliar texture. A strange sense of exposure washed over me, like standing too close to a spotlight. I dropped it instantly, the sound of the card hitting the wooden floor seeming deafening. I backed away from the mailbox, my eyes darting around the street, searching for an observer.

[story 3]

The dull thud of the mailbox lid opening was enough to startle me. When I retrieved the contents, my movements were stiff, almost mechanical. It was a birthday card, addressed to no one, just a simple greeting printed on the front. A knot formed instantly in my stomach, a tight, unpleasant coil. I held it between my thumb and forefinger, the paper feeling strangely significant, loaded with unspoken implications. My gaze flickered from the card to the closed door, then back again, a restless energy vibrating beneath my skin. I didn't want to look at it again, but I couldn't bring myself to discard it. I tucked it into my pocket, the fabric digging slightly into my palm.
